Even with strong experience in AI/ML and Generative AI, getting the right opportunities is not as easy as it looks.

Not because of lack of skills, But because today, everyone is talking about AI… and most profiles start to look the same.

So instead of just saying “I’m looking for opportunities”…
Let me share what I actually do 👇
I work on building real-world Generative AI systems, not just demos.
👉 Systems where users can query complex data using natural language
👉 Systems that reduce hours of manual work into seconds
👉 Systems that don’t just generate answers, but explain them with source data using RAG

Because in real applications, accuracy and trust matter more than hype.
My recent work focuses on:
• LLM-based applications (GPT, Claude, Azure OpenAI)
• RAG pipelines & semantic search
• Agentic AI workflows & automation
• FastAPI-based AI services & microservices
• Cloud platforms (Azure, AWS, GCP)

But honestly… tools are not the real differentiator anymore.
👉 The real value is building AI that actually works in production.

Now coming to the point 👇
I’m actively looking for contract opportunities in:
✔️ Generative AI / LLM Engineering
✔️ AI/ML Engineering
✔️ Data Science (AI-focused roles)
📍 Open to Remote / Hybrid / Onsite
If you’re hiring, or know someone who is, let’s connect 🤝
And even if not, I’d still love to hear what you’re building in AI right now 👇

Thanks,

Robert Half's latest research reveals a striking disconnect. 38% of currently employed workers say they plan to launch a job search in the first half of 2026, up from 29% a year ago. At the same time, the quits rate has been at or below 2.0% for eight consecutive months and recently dropped to 1.9%.

More people want to leave. Almost nobody is actually leaving.

This creates an unusual dynamic. There is a massive pool of workers who are mentally preparing to move but waiting for the right moment. When confidence returns, or when a strong enough offer appears, the volume of active applicants will spike sharply.

The data supports this from the employer side as well. 60% of company leaders plan to increase permanent headcount in the first half of 2026. 55% plan to bring on more contract workers. But 58% say finding skilled talent is harder than a year ago, and 67% of HR leaders report that AI-generated applications are actively slowing their hiring process.

For professionals who are considering a move, the strategic advantage lies in preparation. Getting your resume updated, your target companies researched, and your skills narrative tight before the wave hits puts you ahead of the 38% who are still in the planning phase.

The best time to prepare is when you are not under pressure. Right now qualifies.

Source: Robert Half Hiring Trends H1 2026; BLS JOLTS February 2026

The conversation is dominated by global outrage and anxiety over Oracle’s reported 30,000-person layoff - delivered via early-morning email - perceived as funding aggressive AI infrastructure spending and ongoing H‑1B hiring at the expense of existing staff.

First, there is dispute over whether “AI took the jobs” versus AI serving as a “perfect corporate alibi.” Many posters argue Oracle is not in operating distress, citing a ~95% jump in net income and instead link cuts to $50–58 billion in new debt for AI data centers serving clients like OpenAI and Meta. One influential Spanish-language post frames this as a new pattern: mass firings not because AI replaces work, but to free cash for AI CapEx, with skepticism that AI revenues will cover the debt if the “AI bubble” bursts.

Second, Oracle’s simultaneous filing of over 3,000 H‑1B visa petitions amid layoffs is fueling intense backlash, especially among U.S. tech workers and groups like r/AmericanTechWorkers. Critics frame this as “blatant abuse” of the H‑1B system and call for canceling Oracle government contracts and reforming or eliminating H‑1Bs, while others note the heavy cuts in India (reportedly ~40% in some areas) and worry about knock-on effects in Bangalore and EMEA.

Third, the layoffs are amplifying broader tech-sector insecurity. Workers question whether software careers are “fried,” if firms like IBM are next, and whether to pivot into AI, unionize, or exit tech entirely, using Oracle’s “6 a.m. email” as a symbol of deteriorating employer-employee norms.

Discussion is likely to shift toward hard scrutiny of AI investment ROI, political and regulatory responses to profitable-company layoffs and H‑1B usage, and whether Oracle’s approach becomes a precedent or a reputational cautionary tale in tech.
